title: What is an error in Go
instruction:
In Go, `error` is a built-in type. It is actually an interface with one method: `Error() string`.

When a function can fail, it returns an error as its last return value. If everything went fine, it returns `nil` — Go's way of saying "no error here".

Run this. `strconv.Atoi` converts a string to an integer. It returns both a result and an error.
successMessage: nil means no error. A non-nil error means something went wrong. Two return values, always. That is Go's error contract.

title: Checking an error — if err != nil
hint: if err != nil { fmt.Println("Could not convert:", err); return }  fmt.Println("Result:", n)
expectedOutput: Could not convert: strconv.Atoi: parsing "abc": invalid syntax

codeChecks.message: Check the error: if err != nil { ... }
Print "Could not convert:" when err != nil.
Print "Result:" and n when there is no error.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: if err != nil — you will type this hundreds of times in your Go career. It becomes muscle memory. And it means you never accidentally ignore a failure.

title: errors.New() — create your own error
instruction:
You can create your own error values using `errors.New()`:

`return 0, errors.New("cannot divide by zero")`

The `errors` package is part of the standard library.

Add an error case to `divide`: if `b == 0`, return `0` and a new error `"cannot divide by zero"`. Otherwise return the result and `nil`.
hint: import "errors"  then: if b == 0 { return 0, errors.New("cannot divide by zero") }  return a / b, nil

codeChecks.message: Use errors.New() to create the error.
The error message should be "cannot divide by zero".
Return a/b and nil when b is not zero.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Your own error, your own message. errors.New is the simplest way to create an error. Clean, readable, no setup required.

title: fmt.Errorf() — errors with context
instruction:
`fmt.Errorf` creates an error with a formatted message — like `fmt.Sprintf` but returns an `error`.

`return fmt.Errorf("age %d is invalid: must be between 0 and 120", age)`

This lets you include the actual bad value in the error message, which makes debugging much easier.

Add the error case: if `age < 0` or `age > 120`, return a formatted error. Otherwise return `nil`.
hint: if age < 0 || age > 120 { return fmt.Errorf("invalid age %d: must be between 0 and 120", age) }  return nil
expectedOutput: invalid age 200: must be between 0 and 120

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Errorf() to create the error with the age value.
Include the age value in the error message using %d.
Return nil when the age is valid.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: "invalid age 200" — the bad value is right there in the error. This is how professional Go code reports errors. Always include context.

title: Ignoring errors — the dangerous shortcut
instruction:
You can ignore the error return value with `_`. Go will not stop you.

```
n, _ := strconv.Atoi(input)  // silently ignores any error
```

This is the most common source of subtle bugs in Go. If the conversion fails, `n` will be `0` — and you will never know why.

Run this. Both inputs are processed — but the broken one silently produces 0. Then fix it by printing a message when the error is not nil.
hint: Change n, _ := to n, err :=  then add: if err != nil { fmt.Println("skipping invalid input:", s); continue }

codeChecks.message: Check the error instead of ignoring it with _.
Print a message when an invalid input is found.

successMessage: Total is 40 — 10 + 30, correctly skipping "twenty" with a visible message. Never ignore errors silently. Always handle or at least log them.

title: Multiple error checks in one function
instruction:
A function can have multiple failure points. Check each one and return the first error you find.

`validateUser` below checks three things: name, age, email. Fill in the missing checks.
hint: if name == "" { return errors.New("name cannot be empty") }  if age < 18 { return fmt.Errorf("age %d is too young: must be 18+", age) }
expectedOutput: Invalid: age 15 is too young: must be 18+

codeChecks.message: Check if name is empty.
Check if age is under 18.
Replace the TODO comments with your checks.

successMessage: Four tests, four different outcomes. Each check returns early on the first failure. This is the standard Go validation pattern — return the first error found.

title: Custom error types

codeChecks.message: Return a ValidationError with Field: "email".
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Structured errors with fields you can read programmatically. Not just a message — a type. Next step: check which type of error you got.

title: errors.Is() — identify a specific error
instruction:
When a function can return different errors, you sometimes need to know which one you got.

`errors.Is(err, ErrNotFound)` checks if `err` matches a specific sentinel error.

A sentinel error is a package-level variable: `var ErrNotFound = errors.New("not found")`

Run this. Then add a check for `ErrUnauthorized` and print a different message for it.
hint: } else if errors.Is(err, ErrUnauthorized) { fmt.Println("Access denied — please log in") }

codeChecks.message: Use errors.Is(err, ErrUnauthorized) to check for the unauthorized error.
Print "Access denied — please log in" for the unauthorized case.
Replace the TODO comment with your check.

successMessage: Three IDs, three outcomes, zero crashes. errors.Is lets you handle each failure case differently without string comparison. This is the right way to check for specific errors.

title: errors.As() — extract the error type
instruction:
`errors.As` checks if an error is a specific type and extracts it so you can read its fields.

```
var ve ValidationError
if errors.As(err, &ve) {
    fmt.Println("Field with problem:", ve.Field)
}
```

Run this. The error message alone tells you something went wrong. `errors.As` tells you exactly which field failed.
expectedOutput: Error: age: 200 is out of range
successMessage: errors.As extracted the full ValidationError — field name, message, everything. This is how you build rich error handling that does more than just print a string.

title: Wrapping errors — add context without losing the original
instruction:
When your function calls another function that fails, wrap the error to add context:

`return fmt.Errorf("createUser: %w", err)`

The `%w` verb wraps the original error. The caller gets the full context. `errors.Is` and `errors.As` still work through the wrap.

Run this and see how the error grows as it travels up the call stack.
successMessage: The error message shows the full journey: handleRequest → getUser(42) → database connection failed. errors.Is still finds ErrDatabase through all the wrapping. Context without losing the original.

title: panic and recover
instruction:
`panic` stops normal execution immediately. Use it only for truly unrecoverable situations — programming bugs, impossible states.

`recover` catches a panic inside a deferred function. It is Go's equivalent of a last-chance safety net.

Run this. The `safeDiv` function uses `defer` + `recover` to catch the panic and return 0 instead of crashing.
expectedOutput: Recovered from panic: runtime error: integer divide by zero
successMessage: The program did not crash. recover caught the panic, printed the message, and returned 0. Use panic+recover sparingly — for most errors, return an error value instead.

title: Fix the broken error check
instruction:
This program is supposed to read a number and double it. But it ignores the error — so when the input is invalid it silently doubles 0 and nobody knows why.

Fix it: check the error from `strconv.Atoi`. If there is an error, print `"Invalid input:"` and the error, then return. Otherwise print `"Double:"` and the result.
hint: Change n, _ := strconv.Atoi(input) to n, err := strconv.Atoi(input) then add if err != nil { ... }
expectedOutput: Invalid input: strconv.Atoi: parsing "abc": invalid syntax

codeChecks.message: Capture the error: n, err := strconv.Atoi(input)
Check the error: if err != nil { ... }
Print "Invalid input:" when the error is not nil.

successMessage: 42 for valid input, a clear error message for invalid. No silent failures. This is the difference between code that is easy to debug and code that drives you crazy at 2am.

title: Build a user validator
instruction:
No starter code.

Build a user validator with a custom error type:

1. Define `ValidationError` with `Field` and `Message` string fields and an `Error() string` method
2. Write `validateUser(name, email string, age int) error` that checks:
   - `name` is not empty → `ValidationError{Field: "name", Message: "cannot be empty"}`
   - `age` is between 18 and 120 → `ValidationError{Field: "age", Message: "must be between 18 and 120"}`
   - `email` contains `@` → `ValidationError{Field: "email", Message: "must contain @"}`
3. Test with four users — one valid, three each with a different broken field

Expected output:
```
name: cannot be empty
age: must be between 18 and 120
email: must contain @
Alex is valid
```
hint: Use strings.Contains(email, "@") to check the email. Import "strings".

codeChecks.message: Define a ValidationError struct.
Add Error() string method to ValidationError.
Define the validateUser function.
Check that name is not empty.
Check that age is between 18 and 120.
Check that email contains @ using strings.Contains.

successMessage: A real validator with structured errors. You built it from scratch — custom type, method, three checks, four tests. This exact pattern runs inside production APIs every day.
